data.title: Baker Hughes targets $325M Chart synergies while expecting IET orders to exceed $40B by 2028
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Baker Hughes (BKR) Q1 2026

Management view

  • “Despite a complex operating environment, we delivered another strong quarter of financial results, reflecting the strength of our portfolio and disciplined execution, which more than offset the significant impact of regional disruptions.” (Chairman, President & CEO Lorenzo Simonelli)
  • Simonelli highlighted portfolio actions, saying Baker Hughes “announced the divestiture of Waygate Technologies” and, together with “the sale of PSI to Crane and the joint venture with Cactus,” expects “gross proceeds of approximately $3 billion in 2026.” (Chairman, President & CEO Simonelli)
  • Simonelli emphasized IET demand and backlog visibility, including “bookings reaching a record of $4.9 billion,” IET “book-to-bill of 1.5x,” and “a record RPO of $33.1 billion,” while also stating, “we are increasingly confident that our Horizon 2 IET order target will exceed $40 billion.” (Chairman, President & CEO Simonelli)
  • “As Lorenzo mentioned, we once again delivered strong orders in the first quarter with total company orders of $8.2 billion, including $4.9 billion from IET.” (Executive VP & CFO Ahmed Moghal)

Outlook

  • Moghal guided for Q2 company revenue of $6.5 billion and adjusted EBITDA of $1.13 billion, based on an assumption “the situation in the Middle East will continue through the end of June without further escalation” and that guidance “does not account for any potentially significant secondary impacts, such as elevated inflationary pressures or broader supply chain disruptions.” (Executive VP & CFO Moghal)
  • For Q2 segments, Moghal projected IET EBITDA of $670 million and OFSE EBITDA of $540 million with OFSE revenues estimated at $3.2 billion, adding that Middle East activity assumptions imply the region “potentially falling… more than 20% sequentially.” (Executive VP & CFO Moghal)
  • On full-year 2026, Moghal said Baker Hughes is “maintaining our company's revenue and adjusted EBITDA guidance range” but “anticipate full year results to be slightly below the midpoint,” while adding, “we believe we are well positioned to achieve at least the $14.5 billion midpoint of our order guidance” for IET. (Executive VP & CFO Moghal)
  • Compared with Q4’s macro setup that expected “low single-digit declines in global upstream spending in 2026,” Simonelli said Baker Hughes now expects global upstream spending “to be modestly below our prior outlook of low single-digit declines compared to 2025,” driven by “a significant reduction in Middle East activity.” (Chairman, President & CEO Simonelli)

Financial results

  • Baker Hughes reported Q1 adjusted EBITDA of $1.16 billion and adjusted EPS of $0.58; Moghal also reported GAAP diluted EPS of $0.93 and said Q1 adjusted EBITDA margin was 17.6%. (Executive VP & CFO Moghal)
  • IET results included revenue of $3.35 billion and EBITDA of $678 million, with margin “expanded by 310 basis points to 20.2%,” while Moghal noted IET revenue was “slightly impacted by shipping delays associated with Middle East disruptions across key trading routes.” (Executive VP & CFO Moghal)
  • OFSE revenue was $3.24 billion with EBITDA of $565 million and EBITDA margin of 17.4%; Moghal quantified Middle East disruption as impacting OFSE revenue “by approximately 2% when compared to the fourth quarter of 2025.” (Executive VP & CFO Moghal)
  • Moghal said Q1 free cash flow was $210 million and added it was “further affected by some delays in customer payments,” while reporting net debt to adjusted EBITDA declined to 0.32x and cash increased to $14.8 billion after a debt offering intended to fund the Chart acquisition. (Executive VP & CFO Moghal)

Q&A

  • Arun Jayaram, JPMorgan: Asked for “intermediate and longer-term impact” of the Middle East conflict on infrastructure spend and redundancy; Chairman, President & CEO Simonelli answered that it will “drive fundamental structural change,” including “increased upstream investment,” “rebuilding of global inventories,” and that Baker Hughes “feel good about the opportunity to exceed the $40 billion target for IET orders… in 2028.” (Chairman, President & CEO Simonelli)
  • Scott Gruber, Citi: Asked why Q2 IET guide looks “more flattish” and how OFSE could change with a quicker Middle East recovery; Executive VP & CFO Moghal said the company assumes the conflict persists through June and “the Strait of Hormuz is not fully operational until we enter the second half of the year,” and noted upside could exist but may be delayed because the larger impact is “on the product sales side… given the logistical challenges.” (Executive VP & CFO Moghal)
  • John Anderson, Barclays: Asked about Power Systems drivers and data center demand durability; Chairman, President & CEO Simonelli said Power Systems booked $1.4 billion of orders across “power generation, grid stability and energy management,” and described behind-the-meter as increasingly “long-term baseload power infrastructure.” (Chairman, President & CEO Simonelli)
  • Stephen Gengaro, Stifel: Asked whether portfolio optimization is largely done after Waygate and HMH-related proceeds; Executive VP & CFO Moghal said it is “a continuum” but “in the near term, our focus is very much on closing and successfully integrating the Chart transaction.” (Executive VP & CFO Moghal)
  • Saurabh Pant, BofA: Asked if Baker Hughes is capacity constrained in NovaLT and related equipment; Chairman, President & CEO Simonelli said, “we're effectively sold out of NovaLTs through 2028,” and that the company is “actively assessing capacity needs across our entire Power Systems portfolio.” (Chairman, President & CEO Simonelli)

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts were slightly negative to neutral, pressing for clarity on Q2 guide softness and Middle East recovery timing, including “the guide is a bit more flattish” and questions on whether OFSE could improve sooner. (Scott Gruber, Citi)
  • Management tone was slightly positive but conditional, repeatedly anchoring to assumptions and uncertainty, including “there's still a great deal of uncertainty” and guidance caveats that exclude “potentially significant secondary impacts.” (Executive VP & CFO Moghal)
  • Versus Q4’s more upbeat macro framing, Q1 introduced a more risk-aware posture tied to conflict impacts, with Simonelli describing “a meaningful new layer of macro uncertainty” and highlighting volatility and disruptions. (Chairman, President & CEO Simonelli)

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Q1 shifted the central macro debate from Q4’s broad “geopolitical and trade-related uncertainty” to a specific Middle East conflict scenario with “effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz,” including commentary that “20% of worldwide LNG capacity now offline.” (Chairman, President & CEO Simonelli)
  • Capital structure messaging changed, with Q1 detailing a March issuance of “$6.5 billion in U.S. bonds and EUR 3 billion in European bonds” to fund Chart, versus Q4 emphasizing balance sheet strength ahead of closing. (Executive VP & CFO Moghal)
  • Portfolio actions expanded from Q4’s PSI/SPC completion and $1 billion incremental divestment target to Q1’s Waygate divestiture and expectation of about $3 billion gross proceeds in 2026. (Chairman, President & CEO Simonelli)
  • Power Systems discussion remained a major theme in both quarters, but Q1 added explicit capacity constraints and prioritization language such as “sold out… through 2028” and a focus on customers with “financing and offtake firmly in place.” (Chairman, President & CEO Simonelli)

Risks and concerns

  • Simonelli said the Middle East conflict has “introduced significant volatility,” with disruptions affecting “over 10% of global oil volumes,” and cited inflationary pressures as a potential downside if the conflict persists. (Chairman, President & CEO Simonelli)
  • Moghal cautioned that Q2 and FY assumptions rely on no further escalation and reopening timelines, and warned the guide excludes “elevated inflationary pressures or broader supply chain disruptions.” (Executive VP & CFO Moghal)
  • OFSE execution risk was tied to regional declines and logistics, with Moghal describing Middle East product sales pressure due to “equipment imports and exports,” and saying uncertainty “may impact our ability to achieve the midpoint” of OFSE full-year guidance. (Executive VP & CFO Moghal)

Final takeaway

Baker Hughes management described Q1 as a strong execution quarter led by record IET orders and expanding IET margins, while framing the Middle East conflict and shipping/logistics disruptions as the primary swing factor for OFSE and near-term guide cadence. Management maintained full-year guidance ranges but positioned results slightly below midpoint and repeatedly anchored Q2 expectations to conflict assumptions through June. Alongside the pending Chart close and a stated $325 million synergy target, management underscored continued portfolio monetization with an expectation of about $3 billion in 2026 gross proceeds and reiterated confidence that IET orders can exceed the $40 billion Horizon 2 target by 2028.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: 25% chance the S&P 500 will hit 8,000 in 2026 – Kalshi
data.description:
Prediction markets Kalshi (KALSHI) indicate that about 26.3% of bettors see the probability that the S&P 500 (SP500) may hit 8,000 or above before the end of the year. 

This bet rose from around 10% at the end of March market trough.

 

 

 

Around 61.2% of investors predict that the S&P 500 (SP500) will rise to 7,600 points or above by the end of the year, and about 44% of them believe it could rise to 7,800 points.

The index has reached new intraday highs on Friday—up about 0.8%—and is on track to end at a record. It has advanced 4.7% year-to-date and about 9.5% in the past month.

S&P 500 ETFs: (SPY), (VOO), (IVV), (RSP), (SSO), (UPRO), (SH), (SDS), (SPXU), (FXAIX), (VFIAX), (VFFSX), and (SWPPX).

data.title: Calumet cut at Goldman Sachs after shares triple over the past year
data.description:
Calumet (CLMT) down 5.7% in Friday's trading as Goldman Sachs downgraded the small-cap refiner to Neutral from Buy with a $36 price target, following the stock's outperformance over the last 12 months.

After Calumet (CLMT) shares have more than tripled over the past year, Goldman's Neil Mehta said they now better reflect the improving renewable fuels margin backdrop and management's ongoing commitment to balance sheet strength, where the company has addressed near-term maturities.

The analyst said he remains constructive on the outlook for renewable diesel margins and RIN prices, driven by a more supportive 2026-27 RVO ruling and limited production capacity in the U.S., and he continues to see upside to Calumet's (CLMT) 2027 consensus EBITDA estimates and highlights potential upside scenarios.

For investors looking for small/mid cap refining exposure, Mehta highlighted Buy-rated Delek US (DK) as his preferred equity expression of Goldman's more constructive outlook for RIN prices, where he sees 40% upside potential.

data.title: Catalyst Watch: Earnings blitz, FOMC, Pershing Square IPO, and NYC gets a full casino
data.description:
Welcome to Seeking Alpha's Catalyst Watch - a breakdown of some of next week's actionable events that stand out. Read more about the events that may impact stock prices next week.

 

Monday - April 27

  • Volatility watch - Car rental stocks Avis Budget (CAR) and Hertz Global (HTZ) are set up for another volatile week of trading based on options volume. The most overbought stocks per their 14-day relative strength index include Pitney Bowes (PBI), Avanos Medical (AVNS), and Skillz (SKLZ). The most oversold stocks per their 14-day Relative Strength Index include Northrop Grumman (NOC), Lockheed (LMT), and Tractor Supply (TSCO). Short interest is elevated on SoundHound AI (SOUN) and BigBear.ai Holdings (BBAI) again.
  • Earnings watch - Notable companies due to report include Verizon (VZ), Public Storage (PSA), Nucor (NUE), and Domino's Pizza (DPZ). Options trading implies a double-digit swing for Rambus (RMBS) after it reports.
  • Dividend watch - Companies that have an ex-dividend date coming next week include Cal-Maine (CALM), AGNC Invest (AGNC), Morgan Stanley (MS), and Costco (COST).
  • IPO watch - Pershing Square USA (PSUS) is expected to price its IPO and begin trading. Buyers of the new company will get exposure to Bill Ackman’s investing vehicle plus a small stake in the Pershing Square (PS) management company. The analyst quiet period expires on HMH Holding (HMH) to free up analysts to post ratings.
  • Iran watch - Traders will be on edge once again as weekend developments could impact the conflict in the Middle East and energy prices.
  • All day - Shareholders of Calavo Growers (CVGW) will vote on the proposed merger with Mission Produce (AVO).

 

Tuesday - April 28

  • Earnings watch - Notable companies due to report include Visa (V), Coca-Cola (KO), T-Mobile (TMUS), Booking Holdings (BKNG), Starbucks (SBUX), United Parcel Service (UPS), Robinhood Markets (HOOD), General Motors (GM), Spotify (SPOT), Barclays (BCS), NXP Semiconductors (NXPI), and Seagate Technology (STX). Options trading implies double-digit swings for Porch Group (PRCH) and Bloom Energy (BE) after they report.
  • All day - Resorts World is expected to open the first ever casino with live table games in New York City. The Genting Group property will have a monopoly on NYC gaming until Hard Rock International, backed by New York Mets owner Steve Cohen, builds the Metropolitan Park casino resort adjacent to Citi Field in Queens and Bally's Corporation (BALY) develops its site at Ferry Point near the Whitestone Bridge in the Bronx. Caesars Entertainment (CZR) and MGM Resorts (MGM) could see a negative impact at their Atlantic City casinos from the Big Apple casino rollout.
  • All day - AutoNation (AN) will hold its annual general meeting.

 

Wednesday - April 29

  • Earnings watch - Notable companies due to report include Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL), Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), Meta Platforms (META), AbbVie (ABBV), General Dynamics (GD), Qualcomm (QCOM), and Phillips 66 (PSX). Options trading implies double-digit swings for Wingstop (WING), Carvana (CVNA), and Lemonade (LMND) after their reports are released.
  • All day - Uber (UBER) will hold its annual product showcase in New York City.
  • All day - Coca-Cola (KO) will hold its annual meeting.
  • All day - Shareholders of Peakstone (PKST) will vote on the proposed merger with Brookfield Asset Management (BAM).
  • All day - It is the last business day before the application for the Union Pacific (UNP)-Norfolk Southern (NSC) merger needs to be filed with the Surface Transportation Board.
  • 8:30 a.m. The March Durable Goods Orders will be released by the U.S. Census Bureau.
  • 2:00 p.m. The Federal Reserve will announce its interest rate decision.
  • 2:30 p.m. Federal Reserve Chairman Jerome Powell will hold a press conference.

 

Thursday - April 30

  • Earnings watch - Notable companies due to report include Apple (AAPL), Eli Lilly (LLY), Mastercard (MA), Caterpillar (CAT), Altria (MO), Amgen (AMGN), ConocoPhillips (COP), Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY), and Western Digital (WDC). Options trading implies a double-digit swing for Atlassian (TEAM), Wayfair (W), and Reddit (RDDT) after they report.
  • All day - The Federal Reserve is expected to release its statistical report on finance-company receivables, broken into consumer, real estate, and business credit, with levels, flows, and growth rates.
  • All day - FTSE Russell will hold its Rank Day for the 2026 Russell U.S. reconstitution. The index provider ranks all eligible U.S. stocks by total market capitalization at the market close to determine index membership eligibility for the upcoming reconstitution.
  • All day - Roblox's (RBLX) new Plus subscription priced at $4.99 per month begins.
  • All day - The FDA’s Oncologic Drugs Advisory Committee meets to review AstraZeneca's (AZN) camizestrant in the morning and Truqap in the afternoon, which could matter for biotech and large-cap pharma sentiment.
  • All day - Shareholders of ZIM Integrated Shipping Services (ZIM) will vote on the proposed merger with Hapag-Lloyd (HPGLY). The stock traded 6% below the deal price at the time of publication.
  • All day - Shareholders of Silicon Laboratories (SLAB) will vote on the proposed merger with Texas Instruments (TXN). 
  • 7:30 a.m. The April core PCE price index report will be released. Economists forecast an increase to 3.3% for the core rate.
  • 7:15 a.m. The European Central Bank will release its policy statement.
  • 8:45 a.m. European Central Bank President Christine Lagarde will hold a press conference.

 

Friday - May 1

  • Earnings watch - Notable companies due to report include Exxon Mobil (XOM), Chevron (CVX), and Colgate-Palmolive (CL).
  • Data watch - Key data reports due to arrive during the first few days of the week include updates on monthly deliveries from Nio (NIO), XPeng (XPEV), Li Auto (LI), and BYD Company (BYDDF). Other reports of high interest to investors will be the monthly Macau gross gaming revenue release, the monthly update on Class 8 truck orders, firearm background check data from the FBI, and discount broker DARTs reports.
  • All day - Farmer Brothers (FARM) will hold a special shareholder meeting to vote on the company's merger with Royal Cup.
  • All day - Shareholders of Masimo (MASI) will vote on the proposed merger with Danaher (DHR).
  • All day - Bloomberg will publish its report on oil tanker tracking for April.

 

Saturday - May 2

  • All day - Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) (BRK.B) will hold its first annual meeting without Warren Buffett in his usual role.

 

Seeking Alpha Editor Josh Fineman contributed to this report.

data.title: Ricky Sandler's Eminence Capital to close after 27 years of operation - report
data.description:
Ricky Sandler's Eminence Capital is shutting down after 27 years, Bloomberg News reported, citing a letter written by the CEO.

"Over the last few years, it has become increasingly difficult to apply our rigorous bottom-up investment process to rapidly shifting market conditions and an evolving market structure," Sandler said in the letter seen by Bloomberg.

The hedge fund has posted disappointing returns, and expenses have made it difficult to continue the firm, Sandler reportedly wrote.

Eminence Capital is the latest hedge fund to close, reflecting strain on multi-strategy hedge funds.

Recently, AllianceBernstein (AB) reportedly shut its multi-manager fund AB Arya.

data.title: Ford and Geely reportedly discussed joint venture in U.S. -- WSJ
data.description:
Ford (F) reportedly held talks with China’s Geely (GELYF) (GELHY) to extend a potential European joint venture to the U.S., but has walked away as a collaboration would be “politically fraught.”

According to The Wall Street Journal, Ford (F) was in talks to license Geely’s (GELYF) (GELHY) technology in the U.S. as a way to capitalize on more advanced automobile technology while sidestepping a U.S. ban on Chinese tech.

While Geely (GELYF) (GELHY) supports any avenue into the U.S. market, Ford (F) remains outwardly opposed to any threats to the U.S. auto industry.

Ford CEO Jim Farley has warned that while Chinese-made cars are cheaper, better built, and technologically superior, allowing Chinese automakers to sell cars in the U.S. would be “devastating” to the American auto industry.

“Manufacturing is the heart and soul of our country, and for us to lose that to exports would be devastating,” Farley said as recently as this week.

While talks to collaborate in the U.S. have stalled, Ford (F) and Geely (GELYF) (GELHY) continue to discuss possible joint ventures in Europe.

data.title: S&P 500, Nasdaq on track for another record as U.S. continues ceasefire negotiations with Iran
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages were mixed on Friday as investors digested the latest developments in the Middle East, including a three-week extension to the truce between Israel and Lebanon.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last +0.7%, while the heavy-tech Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was +1.5%. Meanwhile, the blue-chip Dow was -0.3%.

Intel (INTC) shares jumped 20.9% after its Q1 results delivered a beat, and now its second-quarter revenue outlook is expected to surpass what analysts anticipated. Other semiconductors also rose, including Nvidia (NVDA), TSMC (TSM), AMD (AMD), Arm Holdings (ARM), and Qualcomm (QCOM).

Treasury yields were lower. The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was 2 basis points lower at 4.31%, while the 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) dropped 6 basis points to 3.79%.

U.S. President Donald Trump announced that the ceasefire between Israel and Lebanon will be extended for another three weeks after hosting ambassadors of both countries in Washington.

"The meeting went very well! The United States is going to work with Lebanon in order to help it protect itself from Hezbollah. The ceasefire between Israel and Lebanon will be extended by THREE WEEKS," he said via Truth Social.

In addition, Trump is sending envoys Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner to Islamabad this weekend for talks with Iran's foreign minister, according to reports.

On Thursday, Trump also ordered the U.S. Navy to “shoot and kill” any vessel found placing mines in the Strait of Hormuz, adding that there will be "no hesitation."

Moreover, Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps navy laid more mines in the Strait of Hormuz this week, according to a report by Axios.

Crude oil futures (CL1:COM) were slightly lower at $94, while Brent (CO1:COM) was last around $105 per barrel.

On the economy, the Consumer Sentiment Index was revised up in April—to 49.8 from the initial 47.6 and 53.3 in March—according to the University of Michigan Survey of Consumers.

data.title: Trump administration reportedly freezes $344M in crypto allegedly linked to Iran
data.description:
The Trump administration has frozen $344M in cryptocurrency allegedly linked to Iran, CNN reported on Friday, April 24.

Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent reportedly said they are sanctioning multiple wallets tied to the country.

Tether Limited, the company behind the stablecoin Tether USD (USDT-USD), announced yesterday that it has supported the U.S. Government in freezing $344M USDT across two addresses.

The freeze came after information was shared with the company by several U.S. authorities about activity tied to unlawful conduct.

The U.S. Department of the Treasury was not immediately available to comment on the matter.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

Crypto ETFs: (BITW), (BITQ), (GDLC), (BLOX), (STCE), (FDIG), (LFGY), (CRPT), (CEPI), (SATO), (NCIQ), (EZPZ), (TXBC), (QXAS), (TTOP), (BEGS), (KRYP)

data.title: HCA projects 2% to 3% volume growth while holding 2026 outlook amid $600M to $900M exchange headwind
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: HCA Healthcare (HCA) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “The start of the year presented a dynamic environment for HCA Healthcare” as “we did not experience the typical lift related to seasonal respiratory conditions,” with “respiratory-related admissions… down 42%, and… emergency room visits… down 32%,” Samuel Hazen said (CEO & Director Samuel Hazen).

  • Hazen said the shortfall was largely January-specific and “February and March volumes rebounding nicely,” adding, “we experienced a greater net benefit than anticipated from state supplemental programs,” which “mostly offset impacts from the shortfall in volumes” (CEO & Director Hazen).

  • On cost actions and execution, Hazen said teams are “focused on a broad resiliency plan designed to generate cost savings where appropriate, enhance network execution and strengthen organizational capabilities,” and “we expect they will continue to help offset some of the expected impact from the payer mix shift” (CEO & Director Hazen).

  • “For the first quarter, revenue increased 4.3%… adjusted EBITDA increased almost 2% and diluted earnings per share, as adjusted, increased approximately 11% versus the prior year period,” Hazen said, while also pointing to continued investment and capacity adds across the network (CEO & Director Hazen).

  • “Admissions increased 0.9%… equivalent admissions increased 1.3%… outpatient surgeries declined 1.7%… ER visits increased 0.3%,” Mike Marks said, attributing a “drag” from a “much milder respiratory season” and January storms; he said the combined respiratory and storm factors “adversely impacted adjusted EBITDA by an estimated $180 million” (CFO & Executive VP Mike Marks).

Outlook

  • “We believe our assumptions for the remainder of the year related to volumes, payer mix and costs continue to remain in line with our original guidance,” Hazen said, characterizing both the respiratory shortfall and supplemental-payment upside as “first quarter events” (CEO & Director Hazen).

  • Marks said HCA is “reaffirming our estimated guidance ranges for 2026,” while updating a key assumption on Medicaid supplemental payment programs: “We are adjusting our full year range to reflect a decline in supplemental payment program net benefit between $50 million to $250 million versus prior year,” and “this updated guidance does not include any potential impacts from additional approvals of grandfathered applications” (CFO & Executive VP Marks).

  • On the exchange-related headwind, Marks said, “we still believe our full year range of $600 million to $900 million expected impact on adjusted EBITDA is appropriate,” while cautioning “the exchange environment remains dynamic and has not fully settled” (CFO & Executive VP Marks).

  • Compared with the prior quarter’s guidance framing, management maintained the same full-year exchange impact range of $600 million to $900 million and the $400 million resiliency target, while tightening the expected supplemental-payment net-benefit decline to $50 million to $250 million from the prior quarter’s $250 million to $450 million (CFO & Executive VP Marks).

Financial Results

  • Marks said adjusted EBITDA margin “decreased 50 basis points versus prior year quarter,” while “salaries and benefits as a percentage of revenue improved 30 basis points and supplies improved 20 basis points,” and “other operating expenses… increased 90 basis points” driven by items including “cost related to the Medicaid state supplemental payments, professional fees and technological investments” (CFO & Executive VP Marks).

  • On Medicaid supplemental payment programs, Marks said, “While we expected an increase in net benefit of $80 million, we realized an increase in net benefits of approximately $200 million to adjusted EBITDA versus the prior quarter,” citing “the grandfathered approval of Georgia,” “the reinstatement of the ATLIS Program in Texas,” and “the… Tennessee program” (CFO & Executive VP Marks).

  • Capital allocation and liquidity were highlighted with “capital expenditures totaled $1.1 billion,” “we purchased $1.57 billion of our outstanding shares,” “we paid $183 million in dividends,” and “cash flow from operations was $2 billion… a 22% increase” year over year; Marks added leverage remains “in the lower half of our stated target range” (CFO & Executive VP Marks).

Q&A

  • Benjamin Hendrix, RBC Capital Markets: asked how results compared with internal expectations; CFO Marks: said “our results were a bit short… in terms of adjusted EBITDA,” driven by delayed cost flex from the respiratory “ending abruptly” plus winter storm impacts, partially offset by supplemental-payment upside that was “about $120 million higher than our internal expectations” (CFO & Executive VP Marks).

  • Albert Rice, UBS: pressed on unusual-items math and implied cadence to maintain guidance; CFO Marks: said “you’re generally in the zone” and reiterated HCA expects “between 2% to 3% volume growth in the next 3 quarters,” while CEO Hazen added business performance was “pretty much where we expected, save the respiratory dynamic” (CFO & Executive VP Marks; CEO & Director Hazen).

  • Ann Hynes, Mizuho: asked about Florida supplemental-payment timing and exchange bad-debt/collectibility assumptions; CFO Marks: said HCA “feel[s] positive about the prospects of approval” and that if approved Florida “may be significant,” while exchange trends show “growth in patient amounts due” but were within the modeled $600 million to $900 million range (CFO & Executive VP Marks).

  • Benjamin Mayo, Leerink: asked about payer denials/prior auth behavior; CFO Marks: said “increased activity levels… on denials and underpayments,” “call out Medicare Advantage,” and claimed HCA mitigated earnings impact via “recoveries” and “appeals,” while noting payer digital-integration partnerships remain “good in early work products” (CFO & Executive VP Marks).

  • Justin Lake, Wolfe Research: challenged grace-period visibility and revenue recognition; CFO Marks: said premium-status data is “not consistent and is not standardized,” and the company’s “15% drop” estimate includes both known attrition and estimates for those expected to “lose… after the first quarter” (CFO & Executive VP Marks).

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly negative to skeptical, repeatedly testing bridge items, the durability of exchange impacts, Medicaid conversion behavior, and Florida timing, including questions citing “anxiety in the market” and challenges to accounting visibility on exchange grace periods (Ann Hynes, Mizuho: “there are some anxiety in the market”; Justin Lake, Wolfe Research: “my understanding is…”).

  • Management tone was slightly positive and control-oriented in prepared remarks, but more defensive and cautionary in Q&A on policy-linked variability, emphasizing temporality and modeling uncertainty: “we view… as being temporal and not structural,” “it is dynamic,” and “it’s a bit early” (CFO & Executive VP Marks).

  • Versus the prior quarter’s more upbeat close to 2025 (e.g., “strong results” and margin improvement commentary), the current call’s tone leaned more mixed due to respiratory volatility and exchange execution details, while still anchoring to reaffirmed full-year ranges (CEO & Director Hazen; CFO & Executive VP Marks).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The Q4 call emphasized strong momentum, hurricane-market improvement, and introduced 2026 ranges with a $600 million to $900 million exchange headwind and a $400 million resiliency offset, while Q1 focused on variance drivers (respiratory, storms, supplemental payments) and mechanics of exchange attrition and collections (CFO & Executive VP Marks).

  • Guidance posture shifted from initiating 2026 expectations to reaffirming them; the largest explicit assumption change was the supplemental-payment net-benefit decline range moving to $50 million to $250 million versus the prior quarter’s $250 million to $450 million (CFO & Executive VP Marks).

  • Analyst focus remained consistent on exchanges, payer friction, and supplemental payments, but Q1 added deeper scrutiny on grace-period accounting, Medicaid conversion slowdowns, and storm-related demand recapture (CFO & Executive VP Marks).

Risks and Concerns

  • Marks said exchange equivalent admissions declined about 15% and uninsured equivalent admissions increased about 16%, and flagged that exchange reform “remains dynamic and has not fully settled,” with an estimated $150 million adjusted EBITDA impact in Q1 versus prior year (CFO & Executive VP Marks).

  • Marks highlighted a “slowdown of conversions to Medicaid” tied to patients “less willing to fill out Medicaid applications,” and said it is “pretty nascent,” while Hazen said it reflects “dynamics… that we haven’t experienced before” (CFO & Executive VP Marks; CEO & Director Hazen).

  • Hazen described a hurricane-impacted market issue in Western North Carolina where “demand is above our expectation” but “it’s costing us more… [due to] a significant workforce deficit,” leaving results “a little bit behind our expectations… on the bottom line” (CEO & Director Hazen).

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 as a quarter where an unusually mild respiratory season and January storms reduced volumes and delayed seasonal cost flex, while state supplemental programs provided more upside than expected. Leadership reaffirmed full-year 2026 guidance ranges and maintained key planning assumptions on exchanges and resiliency, while updating the expected year-over-year decline in supplemental-payment net benefit to $50 million to $250 million and pointing to potential additional revenue from Florida if approved. The Q&A centered on exchange attrition/grace-period accounting, payer denials intensity, and early signals of slower Medicaid conversions, with management repeatedly emphasizing uncertainty, active monitoring, and confidence in returning to the 2% to 3% volume-growth plan over the remaining quarters.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Stellantis to focus investments on four core brands
data.description:
Stellantis (STLA) is shifting its focus to its four major brands and relegating less profitable brands to “selective investment” as the carmaker’s new CEO moves aggressively to regain momentum and restore profitable growth.

Under the direction of Stellantis CEO Antonio Filosa, Jeep, Ram, Peugeot, and Fiat will now receive the bulk of Stellantis’ investment along with a “material increase” in funding, while the remaining brands like Citroën, Alfa Romeo, Opel, and Maserati will be promoted mostly in regions where the vehicles are more popular, such as France for the Citroën and China for the Maserati.

The new strategy is expected to be unveiled at Stellantis’ Investor Day on May 21.

Formed by the merger between Fiat Chrysler Automobiles and Peugeot S.A. (PSA Group), Stellantis (STLA) owns 14 brands of automobiles, many of which struggle to turn a profit, including Maserati, as well as Jeep and Ram. And in 2025, the company reported a $26.3B loss as a result of EV transition costs, inventory buildup, and canceled orders, including the Ram 1500 EV.  

The EV cost was especially painful for the Netherlands-based automaker. To backtrack from its investment in electric vehicles, Stellantis (STLA) took a charge of ~€22.2B ($26.2B) in the second half of 2025, including ~€6.5B of cash outflows expected over the next four years.

“The charges announced today largely reflect the cost of overestimating the pace of the energy transition that distanced us from many car buyers’ real-world needs,” Filosa said at the time the charge was announced. 

Instead, the company will now focus mainly on hybrids and gas-powered vehicles including a hybrid Jeep Cherokee, Jeep Wagoneer extended-range electric vehicle ((EREV)), and Dodge Charger with a fuel-efficient, turbocharged Hurricane engine. Stellantis (STLA) is also attempting to stop the bleeding by exploring partnerships with Chinese carmakers like Xiaomi (XIACF) (XIACY), Xpeng (XPEV), and Leapmotor (ZGJLY) to jointly build cars that will be sold in Europe and China.

Unfortunately, the shift is doing little to impress investors with Stellantis (STLA) shares down for a fourth straight day.
